text: The laparoscopic splenectomy begins with the patient positioned on his side, right side down. The spleen is visibly enlarged and pushed to the left side, limiting the working space. A twelve millimeter port is used for the camera, with an additional twelve millimeter port and two five millimeter ports inserted for dissection. The surgeon mobilizes the colon by taking down the tissues holding the splenic flexure against the spleen, using an angled scope for visualization.

A five millimeter port is inserted in the flank to aid in retracting the spleen and adjusting instrument angles. The large spleen is lifted from its inferior edge, revealing early attachments around the splenic hilum. The surgeon dissects the lienorenal ligament connecting the spleen to the kidney using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon inserts an additional port to facilitate dissection of the large spleen. An instrument is passed through this port to divide fat at the inferior pole of the spleen. Small splenic tributaries are divided and ligated using the N-seal device, which seals and cuts vessels. The fat is mobilized away from the spleen, exposing tissue between the spleen and stomach, and revealing the splenic hilum where blood vessels enter.

The ENSEAL device is used to open the fat between the spleen and stomach, exposing the lesser sac behind the stomach. The surgeon carefully divides the short gastric vessels running between the stomach and spleen, ensuring no damage to the stomach or bleeding. The posterior short gastric vessels are divided with the ENSEAL device, providing security. Additional tissue near the upper pole is divided to further separate the stomach from the spleen and free the hilum. The peritoneum over the hilum is dissected, revealing the splenic artery and the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses an energy device to divide fatty tissue away from the pancreas, taking care to avoid proximity to the pancreas. The capsule of the hilum of the spleen and the peritoneum surrounding the large blood vessels are dissected. The splenic artery is controlled before the vein to decompress the spleen and facilitate its removal. The artery bifurcates, revealing a branch that supplies the pancreas, which is preserved during dissection. The laparoscopic view shows the spleen forming the roof of the surgical field, highlighting the complexity of the procedure due to the spleen's size.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior aspect of the splenic vein, ensuring no damage to the pancreas. Fatty tissue surrounding the splenic artery is cleaned, and the artery is gradually mobilized. Nerves and lymphatics are meticulously separated from the artery, preparing it for ligation.

The surgeon uses metallic clips to ligate the splenic artery, placing an extra clip for added security to prevent rapid bleeding. The fat surrounding the splenic vein is further mobilized, with careful dissection to avoid potential pancreatic tissue. The posterior hiloperitoneum is divided to isolate the vein, ensuring no damage to the pancreas. The end seal device is used to remove clips from the spleen side of the artery, preparing for stapler use.

The surgeon introduces a vascular stapler to staple the splenic vein, ensuring the staple is securely across the vein. The hilum is fully dissected, and the surgeon turns attention to the remaining parts of the lienorenal ligament, rolling the patient forward to facilitate the spleen's movement. The harmonic scalpel is used to sever the final attachments between the spleen and the diaphragm, freeing the spleen completely. An Alexis extraction bag is then inserted through an enlarged trocar site for spleen removal.

The surgeon enlarges a trocar site from twelve to fifteen millimeters to pass an Alexis extraction bag. The semi-stiff plastic rim of the bag is positioned flat with the posterior aspect tucked behind the inferior pole of the spleen. The anterior part of the hoop is lifted to gradually feed the bag over the spleen, reaching the superior pole. The ring of the bag is then delivered back out through the incision, allowing instruments to morselate the spleen. The splenic tissue is successfully removed without perforating the bag, and the surgical site is checked for bleeding before closure.
